competitors.label: <title>: The Document Title element
competitors.selectionReason: This is the canonical MDN reference for the title element, including browser-tab behavior, accessibility expectations, and search-facing usage notes.
theirStrengths: MDN is authoritative on where title belongs, how it is exposed in browser UI, and why unique concise titles matter.
theirGaps: The reference is less interview-shaped and less explicit about duplicate-title debugging or production scanning failures across many tabs and pages.

realWorldUseCases: It becomes a search-facing headline, a bookmark label, and a debugging clue when many pages look similar.
followUpConfusion: duplicate or vague titles are a common discoverability pitfall.

realWorldUseCases: MDN explains title behavior and accessibility well, but it stays closer to the reference semantics than to one concrete production workflow for title quality.
actionableExamples: It includes title examples and accessibility guidance, so the example criterion stays competitive rather than flipping cleanly to us.
followUpConfusion: Our page is more direct on the follow-up confusion teams hit in practice: duplicate, generic, or brand-first titles that make tabs and results hard to scan.

actionableExamples: Keep the distinct-title practical scenario visible so the page stays faster to use than a longer reference page.

competitors.winReason: MDN is the canonical title reference, but our page is more useful as an interview answer because it turns the title tag into a concrete tab-scanning, search-click, and duplicate-title debugging problem.

competitors.label: HTML Interview Questions and Answers
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd's HTML interview bank is directly relevant because title behavior is a core HTML metadata and discoverability topic in interview prep.
theirStrengths: It is a strong HTML interview-prep hub with broad coverage across document structure and metadata fundamentals.
theirGaps: The category page is not dedicated to title quality, so it is less focused on one exact answer about tabs, bookmarks, and discoverability failures.

realWorldUseCases: It becomes a search-facing headline, a bookmark label, and a debugging clue when many pages look similar.
followUpConfusion: duplicate or vague titles are a common discoverability pitfall.

realWorldUseCases: GreatFrontEnd is relevant for HTML prep, but the category page is less anchored to title-specific production outcomes like tab scanning and duplicate-result confusion.
actionableExamples: It remains a strong interview bank, so the worked-example criterion stays tied rather than flipping automatically to us.
followUpConfusion: Our page answers the likely follow-up more directly by showing why generic titles like Home break both tab clarity and search discoverability.

actionableExamples: Keep the folder-tab mental model and the distinct-title scenario close together so the page stays interview-friendly.

competitors.winReason: GreatFrontEnd is broad HTML prep, but our page is more specific about the title-quality failures and user-facing consequences that make this question matter.

competitors.label: Document structure
competitors.selectionReason: web.dev's document-structure article directly covers the document title as a required head component and explains where titles surface in tabs, history, and search results.
theirStrengths: It is strong on why every page needs a title and on the broader document-shell context around metadata.
theirGaps: It is broader than the exact question because it covers the whole document structure rather than staying tightly focused on title strategy.

realWorldUseCases: It becomes a search-facing headline, a bookmark label, and a debugging clue when many pages look similar.
followUpConfusion: duplicate or vague titles are a common discoverability pitfall.

realWorldUseCases: web.dev grounds titles in real browser and search surfaces, but our answer is more compact about the duplicate-title failures developers actually ship.
actionableExamples: It includes a concrete document skeleton with title placement, so the example criterion stays tied.
followUpConfusion: Our page is clearer on the next interview question: why vague or repeated titles actively hurt scanning, debugging, and click-through.

actionableExamples: Keep the common-mistakes list compact so the page preserves one fast production checklist.

competitors.winReason: web.dev is strong on document structure, but our page is stronger as a short interview answer because it turns the title tag into a concrete discoverability and debugging decision.
